title: People living in democracies and non-democracies
subtitle: [Data](#dod:regimes-polity) by [Polity 5](#dod:polity).
caption:
People living in democracies and non-democracies

Data by Polity 5.

This stacked area chart tracks the global population distribution across four regime types from 1800 to 2018, with the vertical axis measuring population in billions from 0 to 8. The total height of the stacked areas rises from approximately 1 billion in 1800 to nearly 8 billion by 2018. The bottom layer, colored blue and labeled "People living in democracies," remains near zero until the mid-20th century, then expands sharply after 1950, reaching roughly 4.5 billion by 2018. Above it, the purple band for "People living in anocracies" shows modest growth until the 1970s, then accelerates, occupying the space between approximately 4.5 and 5.8 billion in 2018. The red-orange layer, "People living in autocracies," begins as a thin band in 1800, dips slightly around 1940, then surges between 1950 and 2000 before contracting slightly toward 2018, ending near 7.5 billion on the cumulative scale. The topmost gray band, labeled "No regime data," starts as a small segment in 1800, grows gradually through the 19th and early 20th centuries, peaks around 1950 at about 2.5 billion cumulative, then declines steadily to under 0.5 billion by 2018. The visual structure shows that while all categories grow in absolute terms over time, their relative shares shift significantly — particularly the expansion of the democracy category post-1950 and the contraction of the no-regime-data category after mid-century. The chart’s stacking order places democracies at the base, followed by anocracies, autocracies, and finally no regime data at the top.
